coredata.dc:title: Model-free detection and quantitative assessment of micro short circuits in lithium-ion battery packs based on incremental capacity and unsupervised clustering
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Da
Zhang, Meng
Guo, Qiang
Gao, Yibin
Bai, Zhigang
Yang, Qi
Fu, Ke
Lyu, Chao
coredata.dc:description: Timely diagnosis of micro short circuit (MSC) faults is crucial for ensuring the safe operation of lithium-ion battery energy storage systems. Existing diagnostic methods face limitations such as high dependency on battery models, difficulty in determining accurate diagnostic thresholds, or low computational efficiency. This work presents a model-free approach for the detection and quantitative assessment of MSCs in lithium-ion battery packs, with incremental capacity (IC) and unsupervised clustering. First, the IC is extracted from charging voltage data to effectively characterize MSC faults in lithium-ion batteries. Next, principal component analysis is used to map the high-dimensional feature space onto a two-dimensional plane to facilitate fault detection and result visualization. Then, an unsupervised clustering algorithm is employed for anomaly detection to identify MSC cells within the battery pack. For the detected MSC cells, a method based on the maximum charging voltage difference between adjacent cycles is designed to estimate the MSC resistance, quantitatively assessing the severity and evolution stage of the MSC. Experimental results show that the accuracy of MSC detection is 99.17 % and the minimum relative error of short-circuit resistance estimation is 1.20 %, which demonstrates the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Micro short circuit
Incremental capacity
Unsupervised clustering

IC curves of individual cells under different short-circuit resistors.

Fault detection results based on unsupervised clustering.
Charging voltage curves of all cells within the battery pack during the fifth cycle.

head.ce:title: Model-free detection and quantitative assessment of micro short circuits in lithium-ion battery packs based on incremental capacity and unsupervised clustering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: No. 6 Qingnian Road, Yingze District, Taiyuan City, Shanxi Province 030001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Timely diagnosis of micro short circuit (MSC) faults is crucial for ensuring the safe operation of lithium-ion battery energy storage systems. Existing diagnostic methods face limitations such as high dependency on battery models, difficulty in determining accurate diagnostic thresholds, or low computational efficiency. This work presents a model-free approach for the detection and quantitative assessment of MSCs in lithium-ion battery packs, with incremental capacity (IC) and unsupervised clustering. First, the IC is extracted from charging voltage data to effectively characterize MSC faults in lithium-ion batteries. Next, principal component analysis is used to map the high-dimensional feature space onto a two-dimensional plane to facilitate fault detection and result visualization. Then, an unsupervised clustering algorithm is employed for anomaly detection to identify MSC cells within the battery pack. For the detected MSC cells, a method based on the maximum charging voltage difference between adjacent cycles is designed to estimate the MSC resistance, quantitatively assessing the severity and evolution stage of the MSC. Experimental results show that the accuracy of MSC detection is 99.17 % and the minimum relative error of short-circuit resistance estimation is 1.20 %, which demonstrates the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are renowned for their increasing energy density, minimal self-discharge, and extended lifecycle, making them a popular choice for uses like electric cars and grid energy storage systems [1,2]. Nevertheless, due to their poor tolerance to abuse, external environmental conditions, and operational stresses, LIB systems are susceptible to various faults. These faults can accelerate battery degradation and even lead to safety incidents characterized by thermal runaway, raising public concerns about safety [3–5]. Therefore, identifying these malfunctions is crucial for improving the performance of battery systems.

Analysis reports on LIB pack spontaneous combustion accidents from various research institutions over the past few years have indicated that internal short circuits (ISCs) are one of the primary causes leading to explosion in LIBs [6–10]. ISCs typically undergo a prolonged evolution and development process before deteriorating into explosion. In the early stages of ISCs, referred to as micro short circuits (MSCs), the equivalent short-circuit resistance (SCR) is relatively high, resulting in subtle changes in the electrical and thermal parameters of the battery, making early detection challenging [11,12]. At a later stage, ISC detection and warning become relatively straightforward. However, the development from a late-stage ISC to an explosion occurs on a millisecond timescale, rendering control and management interventions ineffective once detection occurs [13]. Therefore, timely diagnosis of early-stage MSCs is crucial and challenging for maintaining the functioning of LIBs.

Existing diagnostic methods for MSCs in LIB packs can be classified into qualitative and quantitative evaluation. [14]. The former involves detecting and locating cells with MSCs within the LIB pack based on terminal voltage correlation [15], cell state of charge (SOC) correlation [16], and charging voltage sequencing [17]. Qualitative detection methods can accurately determine whether an MSC has occurred within a battery pack and pinpoint the MSC cell. Nevertheless, these techniques are unable to measure the short-circuit current (SCC) and SCR quantitatively, thereby lacking the capability to offer a precise assessment of the short circuit's severity.

Quantitative diagnostic methods primarily include charging characteristic methods, representative battery state methods, and Coulomb counting (CC) methods [18–20]. Kong et al. [21] proposed a quantitative diagnostic method for MSCs with remaining charge capacity estimation. Using the charging voltage curve of the cell that remains full charge first as a reference, they transformed the charging voltage curves of other cells with the hypothesis of charging voltage consistency, obtaining the remaining charging time for each cell. This was then converted into the remaining charge capacity for each cell. Subsequently, MSC currents were estimated with the variation in remaining charge capacity. Ma et al. [22] chose the median cell as a representative of normal cells in the LIB pack, using the results from the terminal voltage ranking. They achieved an accurate estimation of the SOC deviation between the "median cell" and the remaining cells using a modified dual extended Kalman filter and the open circuit voltage (OCV)-SOC characteristic curve. Linear fitting of the SOC deviation curve before the inflection point enabled the estimation of SCC and SCR. The results indicate that this method can achieve quantitative detection of MSCs within a single discharge cycle. The method based on CC utilizes an equivalent circuit model (ECM) to establish the battery state space equation, incorporating the MSC current as a system state variable into the established state space equation [23,24]. Filters like the extended Kalman filter or H∞ nonlinear observer are then employed to estimate the battery SOC and SCC. By examining the difference between the SOC estimated by the observer and the SOC calculated by Coulomb counting, the occurrence of an MSC fault in a particular cell can be determined. Since the voltage across the SCRs equals the battery terminal voltage, the aforementioned quantitative diagnostic methods further calculate the SCR using Ohm's law after obtaining the SCC, thereby quantitatively describing the severity of MSC faults.

In conclusion, present quantitative methods depend on the rate of change of SOC deviation of individual cells over time to calculate the SCC and further utilize Ohm's law to compute the SCR. In addition to the aforementioned references, Shen et al. [6] proposed a quantitative diagnostic method based on an ECM and adaptive filtering algorithms, and provided a detailed discussion of the limitations of existing quantitative diagnostic methods. In summary, these existing quantitative diagnostic methods suffer from the following limitations:

Firstly, existing quantitative diagnostic methods require the establishment of an ECM for each individual cell within the LIB pack. The ECM of lithium-ion batteries is difficult to reflect internal reaction mechanisms, and its accuracy is greatly influenced by model parameters. Consequently, the accuracy of SCR estimation is limited by the precision of the model itself and is susceptible to interference from model parameters. Secondly, existing quantitative diagnostic methods require the manual setting of diagnostic thresholds during the process of identifying MSC cells. Lastly, present quantitative methods require the individual calculation of SCCs and resistances for each cell within the LIB pack. Nevertheless, in practical battery packs, the occurrence of multiple cells experiencing MSCs simultaneously is rare and only accounts for a tiny fraction of the total number of cells within the LIB pack [25,26]. Therefore, this indiscriminate individual calculation of short-circuit currents and resistances lacks specificity. For LIB packs consisted of lots of cells, it leads to a significant amount of unnecessary computation, thereby increasing the computational burden on the BMS. Hence, improving the precision and efficiency of MSC detection in LIB packs is an urgent challenge that requires resolution.

In response to the existing issues in current research, this work presents a workflow with incremental capacity (IC) curves and an unsupervised clustering algorithm to achieve MSC detection and quantitative assessment of energy storage LIB packs. Firstly, IC is extracted from the constant current charging stage and smoothed using a moving average filter to obtain features that effectively characterize MSC faults in LIBs. Then, to facilitate fault detection and visualization, the high-dimensional IC curves are mapped onto a two-dimensional plane using principal component analysis (PCA). Next, using the first two principal components of the IC curves as inputs, an unsupervised clustering algorithm is employed to detect MSC cells within the LIB pack. For the detected fault cells, the SCC and SCR are quantitatively calculated based on the maximum charging voltage (MCV) difference between adjacent charge-discharge cycles. Diagnosis results demonstrate that the presented method can effectively detect MSC cells within the battery pack and accurately calculate their SCR to quantify the evolution stage and severity of MSCs. The contributions of the presented workflow can be summarized as follows:
(1)
The presented workflow achieves MSC diagnosis for LIB packs without the need to establish a battery model, thus circumventing the challenging issue of accurate model development.
(2)
The proposed method does not require setting diagnostic thresholds, avoiding the challenge of determining precise thresholds.
(3)
The presented method adopts a technical route of first detecting MSC cells and then quantitatively estimating their SCR. This approach of detecting first and then performing quantitative calculation is highly targeted, avoiding unnecessary computations and improving diagnostic efficiency.

The flowchart of the diagnosis workflow is shown in Fig. 1. As depicted in Fig. 1, the implementation process of the diagnosis workflow consists of five main steps: data acquisition, IC curve extraction, feature dimensionality reduction based on PCA, MSC detection based on an unsupervised clustering algorithm, and SCR estimation based on the MCV difference between adjacent cycles. In practical applications, the battery management system can directly acquire the current and cell terminal voltage data required by the proposed method. Therefore, the data acquisition step does not require further elaboration here. The subsequent sections will provide a detailed explanation of the remaining four main steps.

In this subsection, the IC is extracted from the voltage curve at a constant current charging phase and utilized as a feature to characterize MSC faults in LIBs. IC analysis is a non-destructive technique for identifying degradation mechanisms in lithium-ion batteries [27]. The IC curve is typically obtained using numerical differentiation, with the calculation formula as follows:
(1)
I
C
EVI
=
d Q
d V
≈
Δ Q
Δ V
=
Q
2
−
Q
1
Δ V
where Q represents the battery's charging capacity and V is the terminal voltage during constant current charging; where Q 2-Q 1 denotes the charging capacity change over the voltage interval ΔV. To fully capture the features of the IC curve, ΔV is set to 1 mV.

Subsequently, a moving average filter (MVF) is used to smooth the IC curve. Given a time-varying signal s with noise, the MVF is formulated as follows:
(2)
s ˆ
k
=
1
2 ⋅
N
p

j = −

p
N
p
s
k − j
where s
k represents the value of the original signal s at the k th time step, and
s ˆ
k
is the corresponding filtered value. The window size of the MVF is 2 N
p +1 and
N
p
∈
ℕ
+
, where N
p is set to 15 as referenced in [28].

We consider the IC curve extracted from each individual cell during one charge-discharge cycle as a sample. For all cells within the LIB pack, their data matrix after IC extraction is represented as follows:
(3)
C =

where c
i_j ∈ℝ m×1 denotes the IC extracted from the i th cell during the j th cycle, i=1, 2, …, n；j=1, 2, …, d. n represents the number of cells within the battery pack, and d indicates the number of charge-discharge cycles.

First, we compute the covariance matrix of the data matrix C as shown in the following equation:
(4)
R =
1

ℝ

Furthermore, by solving Eq. (5), the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the covariance matrix are obtained, thereby determining the load matrix.
(5)
|
R − λ
I
m
|
= 0
where I
m is the m-dimensional identity matrix. The eigenvalues λ =(λ 1, λ 2, …, λ
m ) are computed, where λ 1≥λ 2≥…≥λ
m . The matrix of eigenvectors corresponding to the eigenvalues, denoted by P =( p
1, p
2, …, p
m ), serves as the load matrix of C . Each column of P is termed a load vector, and they are mutually orthogonal to each other. Therefore, the data matrix C can be decomposed as follows:
(6)
C =
∑
i = 1
q

⊤

C ˜

C ˜
where S ∈ℝ nd×q is the score matrix, and each column vector s
i = Cp
i from S represents the i th principal component, essentially the projection of matrix C along the direction of the load vector;
C
̃
represents residuals, q represents the number of principal components and satisfies q<m. In this study, the first two principal components in the principal component subspace are selected to represent the original data matrix.

Density-based spatial clustering of applications with noise (DBSCAN) is a commonly used and efficient clustering method, characterized by its minimal requirement for hyperparameters, high robustness to cluster shapes, and insensitivity to noise compared to other clustering algorithms [29]. It is particularly suitable for density clustering tasks where the number and shape of clusters are unknown in advance. Due to its reliance on the distribution density of data points in feature space, DBSCAN typically labels points in regions of low density as outliers, enabling the algorithm to identify anomalous points within the dataset. Therefore, we construct a fault detection model with the DBSCAN algorithm to achieve accurate detection of MSC cells within lithium-ion battery packs. Specifically, we use the first two principal components of the IC curves obtained in Section 2.2 as classification features, which are input into the DBSCAN algorithm to detect MSC cells.

The core idea of DBSCAN is to partition regions with sufficient density into clusters. The algorithm has two parameters: the neighborhood radius ε and the density threshold MinPts. It categorizes all points in the sample set X into core points, density-reachable points, density-connected points, and outliers, defined as follows:
•
If a point k has at least MinPts points including itself within a distance of ε, then it is considered a core point, and all points within the range ε are deemed directly density-reachable from k.
•
If there exists a sequence of points k 1, k 2, …, k
n , and each k
i+1 is directly density-reachable from k
i , then k
n is deemed density-reachable from k 1。
•
Given objects a, k, b∈X, if both k and b are density-reachable from a with respect to ε and MinPts, then k and b are considered density-connected with respect to ε and MinPts.
•
Points that are not density-reachable from any other point are referred to as outliers.

The principle of the DBSCAN algorithm is illustrated in Fig. 2. Here, MinPts is set to 5, with orange points denoting core points, which have at least five samples within their neighborhood radius ε. All points within the range ε of a core point are considered directly density-reachable from that core point. Core points that are not in the same hypersphere are density-reachable. A sequence of core points connected by solid black arrows forms a density-reachable sequence. All samples within the range ε of a density-reachable sequence are density-connected. Gray points, not within the range ε of any core point, i.e., not density-reachable from any point, are hence considered outliers.

Compared to other clustering algorithms, DBSCAN does not require a predefined number of clusters. Moreover, it performs clustering based on point cloud density, making it capable of identifying clusters of any shape, thus offering greater flexibility. This makes it suitable for fault detection scenarios where the number and characteristics of the targets are unknown, such as in the complex and dynamic operational environments of energy storage lithium-ion battery systems. Additionally, by setting the density threshold parameter MinPts, DBSCAN effectively filters out low-density environmental noise points, thus minimizing misdiagnosis. Furthermore, DBSCAN is almost insensitive to the traversal order of points, and it maintains stable clustering performance under different initialization conditions.

When an MSC occurs within a LIB cell, electrons pass through the separator internally, leading to self-discharge and internal consumption of charge. In other words, an MSC manifests primarily as the self-discharge phenomenon in LIBs. This process is equivalent to connecting a resistive load across the terminals of the cell. Consequently, the equivalent SCR can be used to quantitatively assess the self-discharge capacity and thereby evaluate the severity of the MSC.

Specifically, this process can be intuitively illustrated using the ECM of a LIB, as shown in Fig. 3, where U oc, R
i , I L, I, I MSC, and R MSC represent the open-circuit voltage, internal resistance, external load current, internal current, SCC caused by the MSC, and SCR, respectively. As depicted, the direction of the leakage current I MSC remains the same during both the charging and discharging processes. Consequently, regardless of whether the battery is charging or discharging, the SCR consistently acts as a load, continuously consuming the battery's energy.

We consider a series-connected battery pack undergoing a constant current (CC) charge and dynamic stress test (DST) discharge. The voltage profiles over two consecutive cycles are illustrated in Fig. 4(a). Here, CCV denotes the charging cut-off voltage. The gray solid line represents the terminal voltage curve of a normal cell within the LIB pack, while the pink solid line indicates the terminal voltage curve of a cell experiencing a MSC. As shown in Fig. 4(a), during both the charging and discharging phases, the MSC cell discharges through the SCR, which continuously consumes the energy of the MSC cell. This results in the terminal voltage of the MSC cell being lower than that of the normal cell.

Furthermore, we examine the charging voltage curves of the MSC cell over two consecutive cycles in isolation, as depicted in Fig. 4(b). The figure illustrates that the maximum charging voltage (MCV 2) during the second cycle (Cycle 2) is significantly lower than the maximum charging voltage (MCV 1) during the first charge-discharge cycle (Cycle 1). This discrepancy in the maximum charging voltage between adjacent cycles is also attributed to the additional energy consumption by the SCR in the MSC cell. Assuming that at the end of the second charging cycle of the battery pack, the MSC cell is removed from the pack and charged individually until its maximum charging voltage matches that of the first charging cycle, as indicated by the green dashed line in Fig. 4(b). The charging time required at this point, denoted as Δt, is defined as the remaining charging time of the MSC cell. In the practical operation of energy storage LIB packs, it is impractical to remove a MSC cell and charge it individually to determine the residual charging time Δt. Consequently, the remaining charging time Δt is theoretically unknown. However, it can be observed that if we interpolate the maximum charging voltage (MCV 2) of the subsequent cycle on the charging voltage curve of the previous cycle and denote the corresponding time as t, then the time interval from t to the end of the charging in the previous cycle is equal to the remaining charging time Δt. Therefore, by interpolating the maximum charging voltage of the current cycle on the charging voltage curve of the previous cycle, the remaining charging time Δt for the MSC cell in the current cycle can be obtained.

Upon determining the remaining charging time Δt for the MSC cell in the current charge-discharge cycle, multiplying it by the charging current of the LIB pack yields the energy loss of the MSC cell in the current cycle. This energy loss is attributed to the SCR, expressed as follows:
(7)
Q
l o s s , i
= I ⋅ Δ t
where Q
loss,i represents the energy loss of the MSC cell at the end of the i th charging cycle, and I denotes the charging current. The rate of change of energy loss over time corresponds to the SCC of the MSC cell, thus:
(8)
I
MSC
=
Q
l o s s , i
−
Q
l o s s , i − 1
T
i
−
T
i − 1
where I MSC represents the MSC current, Q
loss,i is the estimated energy loss at the end of the i th charging cycle, and Q
loss,i−1 is the estimated energy loss at the end of the (i−1)th charging cycle. T
i and T
i−1 denote the times at the end of the i th and (i−1)th charging cycles, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 3, the voltage across the equivalent SCR R MSC is equal to the battery terminal voltage. Therefore, after calculating the SCC I MSC, we further compute the SCR using Ohm's law. Specifically, since the terminal voltage of the MSC cell varies between the end of charging in two adjacent cycles, we use the average terminal voltage between these two moments to calculate the SCR, as described in the following equation:
(9)
R
MSC
=
U
M
I
MSC
where R MSC represents the MSC resistance, and U
M denotes the average voltage between the end times of two adjacent charge cycles. A smaller SCR results in higher heat generation within the battery, thereby increasing the likelihood of thermal runaway.

Given that the heat generated by MSCs in batteries is low and that the thermal management system can effectively dissipate heat, the thermal effects of MSCs can be neglected. Therefore, this study adopts the approach of connecting resistors in parallel across the LIB to simulate MSC faults. This method not only enables better control of the triggering time and location of MSCs but also simulates the development of MSCs, offering good reproducibility.

Fig. 5 depicts the experimental setup. All experiments were conducted in a controlled temperature environment maintained at 25 °C by a constant temperature chamber. The host computer managed the battery testing system for charging and discharging the LIBs. Data was sampled at a frequency of 1 Hz. The LIB pack used in the experiment comprised 8 cells connected in series, with the specifications of the individual cells provided in Table 1. Due to user habits, lithium-ion battery packs typically do not undergo full discharge in practical applications, meaning the initial SOC of each cell in the actual battery pack is generally not zero. Therefore, during fault simulation and data collection, we set the initial SOC of each cell to 20 % in the first cycle.

Constant current charging at 0.5 C and DST discharging were executed on the LIB pack during the experiment. The voltage and current curves of the battery pack during a certain charge-discharge cycle are shown in Fig. 6.

The battery pack underwent a total of 15 charge-discharge cycles. To simulate the evolution of MSCs, different magnitudes of SCRs and corresponding manual switches were connected in series and then in parallel across cells 4 and 8. The parallel SCRs for each cycle are shown in Table 2. It can be observed that during the experimental process, all switches are in the open state during the first three charge-discharge cycles of the battery pack, indicating the absence of MSCs. From the 4th to the 15th charge-discharge cycle, the corresponding switches are closed sequentially to connect SCRs in parallel with the battery terminals from largest to smallest, simulating the gradual worsening of MSCs as the cycles progressed. Specifically, for each individual charge-discharge cycle, the SCR is connected when the battery pack began charging and disconnected when the battery pack stopped discharging. The battery pack is then allowed to rest for 30 minutes before starting the next cycle. In other words, the 15 charge-discharge cycles are not continuous. There is a 30-minute rest period between adjacent cycles during which no SCR is connected.

The charging voltage curves of the individual cells within the battery pack over 15 cycles are shown in Fig. 7. As can be seen, cells 4 and 8, which exhibit MSC characteristics, are unable to reach full charge. This phenomenon becomes increasingly pronounced as the SCR decreases. The SOC curves of individual cells over 15 charge-discharge cycles are shown in Fig. 8. As seen in Fig. 8, starting from the 4th cycle, the SOC of MSC cells 4 and 8 is lower than that of the normal cells, and this trend becomes increasingly pronounced as the SCR decreases. Moreover, by combining Fig. 7 and Fig. 8, it can be observed that from the 4th to the 15th cycle, the incomplete charging of the MSC cells and the incomplete discharging of the normal cells become progressively more evident. During cycles 4–15, the short-circuit current variation curves for MSC cells 4 and 8 are shown in Fig. 9. From Fig. 9, it can be observed that the short-circuit currents of cells 4 and 8 exhibit a consistent trend and gradually increase over time.

The IC curves are depicted in Fig. 10, from which it can be observed that starting from the fourth cycle, the IC curves of MSC cells 4 and 8 exhibit a leftward shift in their starting points and peak positions compared to those of the other cells, and this shift increases with the reduction in the SCR. This phenomenon occurs because the SCR continuously consumes energy in MSC cells during both charging and discharging processes. Consequently, the voltage of MSC cells increases more slowly during charging compared to normal cells. When the terminal voltage of the normal cells reaches the cutoff voltage, the battery pack stops charging to prevent overcharging, leaving the MSC cells undercharged with a terminal voltage lower than that of the normal cells. During discharging, the terminal voltage of the MSC cells decreases faster than that of the normal cells. When the terminal voltage of the MSC cells reaches the discharge cutoff voltage, the battery pack stops discharging to prevent over-discharge. At this point, the normal cells remain partially discharged with a terminal voltage higher than that of the MSC cells. This disparity becomes increasingly pronounced with each cycle and is exacerbated by a reduction in SCR. This further results in the initial terminal voltage of the normal cells being higher than that of the MSC cells at the start of each charging cycle, causing the IC curves of the MSC cells to shift leftward relative to those of the normal cells. Similar conclusions can be drawn from the IC curves of individual cells obtained from the 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th, 13th, and 15th charge-discharge cycles. For brevity, this paper refrains from reiterating these observations. Additionally, it can be observed that the IC curves in Fig. 10(a) differ from those in Fig. 10(b). This discrepancy is caused by the difference in initial SOC between the two cycles. Specifically, in the first cycle, the initial SOC of each cell was set to 20 %, while in the second cycle, it was 0 %.

The results after PCA dimensionality reduction are shown in the Fig. 11 below. The horizontal axis represents the first principal component, while the vertical axis represents the second principal component. As depicted, the cumulative variance explained by the first two principal components is 87.3 %. This indicates that the first two principal components effectively characterize the MSC fault characteristics of the LIB pack, facilitating DBSCAN-based fault detection.

The MSC detection results based on DBSCAN clustering algorithms are illustrated in Fig. 12. In the figure, blue circles represent normal samples, while yellow crosses indicate detected fault samples. As previously explained, we treat the features extracted from each individual cell in one cycle as a sample. Hence, for the battery pack used in the experiment, a total of 120 samples are obtained from the 8 cells over 15 charge-discharge cycles. Each sample is labeled as v_i_j, corresponding to the features of the i th cell in the j th charge-discharge cycle, where i=1, 2, …, 8 and j=1, 2, …, 15. Moreover, to provide a clearer presentation of the fault detection results, we only annotate the sample numbers of the detected fault samples in Fig. 12, without displaying the sample numbers of the numerous normal samples.

As shown in Fig. 12, all normal samples are concentrated within a high-density region. More importantly, during the 4th to 15th cycles, the majority of samples from Cell 4 and Cell 8 are diagnosed as faulty samples. Additionally, as the equivalent SCR connected across the cells decreases, the distance between the faulty samples and the high-density region where normal samples reside increases progressively. It is noteworthy that the faulty sample v_4_5 is misdiagnosed as a normal sample, which could be attributed to the measurement noise of the sensors in the data acquisition system. Specifically, the charging voltage curves of each cell within the battery pack during the fifth cycle are shown in Fig. 13. As shown in Fig. 13, by retrospectively examining the charging voltage curves of each cell within the battery pack during the fifth cycle, we observe obvious measurement noise in the charging voltage curve of the MSC cell 4 compared to other cells. This measurement noise causes the charging voltage curve of cell 4 to close that of the normal cells. Consequently, the principal component of the IC curve of the MSC cell 4 in the fifth cycle becomes very similar to that of the normal cells. This ultimately results in the proposed method misdiagnosing the MSC cell 4 as a normal cell in the fifth cycle. In other words, the misdiagnosis of v_4_5 as a normal cell is due to measurement errors in the voltage sensor corresponding to cell 4 during the fifth cycle.

Despite the occurrence of a tiny proportion of misdiagnoses, the accuracy (the number of correctly detected samples divided by the total number of samples) of the MSC detection method proposed in this paper remains as high as 99.17 %. In conclusion, the aforementioned fault detection results align closely with the fault settings outlined in Section 3, demonstrating the capability of the proposed method to accurately detect MSC cells in LIB packs and affirming the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

Next, we further estimate their SCRs, aiming to quantify the severity and evolution stage of the MSC. The true values, estimated values, and relative errors of the SCRs for MSC Cells 4 and 8 are listed in Table 3. As shown in Table 3, for MSC Cell 4, the maximum relative error of the estimated SCR is 3.85 %, while for MSC Cell 8, it is 4.26 %. Furthermore, it can be observed from Table 3 that as the true SCRs connected across the cells decrease, the relative errors of the estimation results exhibit a decreasing trend. This phenomenon arises because with the decrease in SCR, the SCC gradually increases, making the fault characteristics caused by the MSC more apparent. The estimation errors of the SCRs mentioned above are within an acceptable range, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method for the quantitative assessment of MSCs.

As discussed in the introduction section of the manuscript, existing MSC fault diagnosis methods can be categorized into qualitative detection and quantitative diagnosis. The workflow presented in this work takes a fundamentally different technical route, being data-driven in nature. Therefore, we compared our method with existing methods from four aspects: whether the MSC is detected first, whether the SCR can be quantitatively estimated, whether a battery model needs to be established and whether diagnostic thresholds need to be set. The comparison results are summarized in Table 4 below.

As shown in Table 4, qualitative detection methods cannot quantitatively calculate SCC and SCR, thereby failing to provide a quantitative description of the short-circuit severity. Since they cannot preemptively detect MSCs within the battery pack, quantitative diagnostic methods must individually calculate the SCR for each cell. However, in a battery pack composed of hundreds or thousands of cells, only a very small fraction of cells experience MSCs simultaneously. Consequently, the undifferentiated individual calculations required by existing quantitative diagnostic methods result in a substantial amount of unnecessary computation, thereby increasing the computational burden on the battery management system. Furthermore, as shown in Table 4, the existing methods may also face the following limitations: (1) They require an accurate equivalent circuit model for each cell in the battery pack. However, the equivalent circuit model of a lithium-ion battery is limited in its ability to reflect the internal reaction mechanisms of the battery, and its accuracy is highly dependent on the model parameters. This means that the accuracy of fault detection is constrained by the model’s precision and is susceptible to interference from model parameters. (2) Determining accurate thresholds is challenging. In existing methods, the threshold directly impacts the accuracy and reliability of fault detection. Establishing an accurate diagnostic threshold often necessitates extensive offline testing, which is time-consuming and hinders the online application of the diagnostic method.

Compared to existing methods, the advantages of the proposed method in this work are as follows: (1) It achieves MSC diagnosis in lithium-ion battery packs without the need to establish a battery model or set diagnostic thresholds, thereby avoiding the challenges of creating accurate models and determining precise thresholds. (2) The proposed method accomplishes MSC detection and quantitative estimation of SCR. This approach of detecting first and then performing quantitative calculations is highly targeted, reducing unnecessary computations and improving diagnostic efficiency.

Additionally, similar to the proposed method, the approach based on remaining charge capacity (RCC) described in [21] and the method utilizing incremental capacity differences (ICD) between normal and MSC cells in [25] also do not require the establishment of a battery model for SCR estimation. Therefore, we further compared our method, which is based on the maximum charging voltage difference between adjacent cycles, with these two benchmark methods. Specifically, the SCR estimation results for the two benchmark methods are shown in Table 5.
Figs. 14 and 15 provide a visual comparison of the relative errors in SCR estimation for cell 4 and cell 8, respectively, using the three methods. A comparison of Table 3 and Table 5, along with Fig. 14 and Fig. 15, demonstrates that for both cell 4 and cell 8, the proposed method consistently yields the smallest relative error in SCR estimation, further validating the effectiveness and accuracy of the proposed approach.

This paper proposes a workflow for diagnosing MSC faults in energy storage LIB packs based on IC analysis and unsupervised clustering algorithms. IC curves are extracted from charging voltage data to characterize the MSC features of LIBs. By integrating PCA and DBSCAN clustering algorithms, MSC cells are accurately detected. For the detected MSC cells, their SCRs are quantitatively estimated based on the maximum charging voltage difference between adjacent cycles. This serves as a key indicator to assist the BMS in assessing the severity and evolution stage of MSCs and facilitates the implementation of targeted preventive maintenance measures. The fault detection and quantitative evaluation results reveal that the IC curves of MSC cells exhibit different trends compared to those of normal cells. The accuracy of the proposed MSC detection method is 99.17 %. The maximum relative error of the estimated SCRs is 4.26 %. Since the proposed method does not require the establishment of battery models or the manual setting of diagnostic thresholds, and follows a technique route of detection followed by quantitative calculation, it possesses better applicability and computational efficiency.

Due to the fact that the inherent inconsistency among cells within the battery pack exhibit similar characteristics with MSCs, there is a possibility of misdiagnosing inconsistency as MSCs. In future work, we will focus on considering the impact of inconsistency to further refine the proposed method. Furthermore, due to constraints on experimental time and costs, we have validated the proposed method using only the DST conditions, which are representative and widely employed in existing research. In future studies, a key focus will be to verify the effectiveness of the proposed method under other types of dynamic conditions. It should be noted that the proposed method is not applicable to charging modes with varying current, such as constant voltage charging, pulse charging, and constant power charging, which represents one of its limitations. To address this limitation, a potential solution is to treat the battery’s voltage and current data as time series and extract a range of statistical features to characterize MSC faults, serving as an alternative to IC curves. This will be a primary focus of our future research efforts.

Da Lei: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Meng Zhang: Software, Project administration, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhigang Bai: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Qi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Qiang Guo: Supervision, Investigation. Yibin Gao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Conceptualization. Ke Fu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration. Chao Lyu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the project from State Grid Shanxi Electric Power Company [Grant No. 520530240027].
